preFix.title: Slow-roast shoulder of pork
preFix.subtitle: Eight hours at 130 degrees C, pulled apart at the table
preFix.excerpt: A whole bone-in pork shoulder roasted for eight hours at very low heat until the meat pulls apart in chunks with two forks. The most relaxed roast in this collection: the oven does everything.
preFix.sourceNotes: Extreme slow-roast method. A lower and longer cook than the standard shoulder roast, producing a completely fall-apart result distinct from the 5-hour sliceable version.

content.text: This is different from the five-hour shoulder in one important respect: at eight hours at 130 degrees C, the meat does not slice. It falls apart completely. Pull it with two forks at the table. The crackling forms during the initial high-heat blast and stays crisp as the temperature drops, sitting on top of an increasingly tender mass of falling meat underneath.

items.name: Pork shoulder
items.prepNote: bone-in, skin scored at 1 cm intervals

items.name: Garlic
items.prepNote: halved, pushed into the score cuts

content.text: Score and season the night before

Score the skin at 1 cm intervals cutting through to the fat. Push a garlic half into every other cut. Rub salt and crushed fennel over the skin and into the cuts. Leave uncovered in the fridge overnight.

Remove the pork from the fridge 1 hour before cooking. Heat the oven to 230 degrees C fan. Scatter the onion in the base of a deep roasting tin and pour in the cider. Sit the shoulder on top, skin-side up. Roast at 230 degrees C for 25 minutes until the
Reduce the oven to 130 degrees C fan. Cover tightly with foil or a lid and
for 8 hours. Check at 4 hours and add 200 ml of hot water if the base of the tin is drying out.

Remove the foil for the last 30 minutes to allow the crackling to re-crisp. The meat should pull apart with absolutely no resistance. Rest for 30 minutes, then serve by pulling the meat apart at the table with two forks.

items.fix: Remove the foil for the last 30 to 45 minutes and raise the oven to 200 degrees C fan. The crackling should re-crisp quickly.
items.cause: Condensation from the covered tin dripped onto the crackling.
items.symptom: The crackling softened during the 8-hour covered cook

items.fix: Continue cooking at 130 degrees C for another hour. A 3 kg joint needs the full 8 hours from room temperature; a cold joint needs more.
items.cause: A very large, cold joint, or the foil seal was poor and the temperature dropped.
items.symptom: The meat is still firm and has not pulled apart after 8 hours

items.fix: Always check at 4 hours and top up with hot water or cider. A good seal is more important than the liquid amount.
items.symptom: The cooking liquid has evaporated and the base is catching

items.fix: This is normal with a bone-in shoulder. Pull the meat apart and separate the large fat chunks by hand. The remaining fat is the flavour.
items.cause: A very fatty shoulder was used.
items.symptom: The fat in the pulled meat is excessive

content.text: Replace the cider with apple juice for a sweeter, more family-friendly version. Add a handful of fresh thyme to the base of the tin. The pulled pork works brilliantly in rolls with coleslaw and barbecue sauce the day after.

The extreme slow-roast pork shoulder is a technique that came from American pulled pork barbecue culture and was adopted into British home cooking in the early 2000s. The method suits British pork beautifully because it produces a result that is the opposite of the dry, overcooked pork that the generation before had learned to expect. It is now a British Sunday lunch staple in its own right.
